While White has a slight space advantage, Black's piece coordination with the active c6 bishop and the solid pawn structure make it nearly impossible to break down.
variations.shortExplanation: Fort Knox ...Bd7-c6 places the bad bishop on its best diagonal; symmetrical structure, hard to crack.

variations.name: Advance: Milner-Barry Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Qb6 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Bd7 O-O Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc3 a6 Qe2 Ne7 Kh1 Nc6 f4 Nb4 Rd1 Nxd3
variations.explanation: The Milner-Barry Gambit sees White sacrifice the d4 pawn with Bd3 and O-O for rapid development and attacking chances. Black accepts the pawn with ...Nxd4 followed by ...Qxd4, grabbing material while staying alert. After Nc3 a6 Qe2 Ne7 the knight reroutes back to c6 and ...Nb4 attacks the d3 bishop. ...Nxd3 simplifies decisively into a winning endgame — Black's extra pawn plus the bishop pair settles the position.
variations.shortExplanation: Milner-Barry: accept d4 pawn, consolidate with ...a6/Ne7-c6, then ...Nb4/Nxd3 simplifies into winning endgame.

name: Caro-Kann Defence
pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 Nc3 dxe4 Nxe4 Bf5 Ng3 Bg6 h4 h6 Nf3 Nd7 h5 Bh7 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 e6 Bf4 Ngf6 O-O-O Be7 Ne4 O-O Kb1 c5
overview: The Caro-Kann is named after German amateur Horatio Caro and Austrian master Marcus Kann who independently analyzed the opening in the 1880s. It became respectable when Capablanca made it his preferred drawing weapon in the 1920s; Tigran Petrosian raised it to championship status in the 1960s with his impenetrable defensive treatment; Anatoly Karpov played it through his entire World Championship career. The Caro is the ultimate solid defence against 1.e4 — you get a healthy pawn structure AND your light-squared bishop develops outside the pawn chain before …e6 (this is the key advantage over the French). Positions tend to be strategic and long-term, but don't be fooled — there's plenty of venom in the sharper lines.
keyIdeas: Develop the light-squared bishop to f5 or g6 BEFORE playing ...e6
Maintain a solid pawn structure with pawns on c6, d5, and e6
Play for endgame advantages — the structure is inherently healthy
Use ...Nd7-f6 or ...Ngf6 development to control the center
traps: In the Classical, after h4 h6 h5 Bh7 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3, White has spent tempi pushing h-pawns while Black develops smoothly
In the Advance, ...c5 breaks down White's center quickly and Black can win the d4 pawn if White overextends
warnings: In the Fantasy Variation (f3), White sacrifices a pawn for rapid development — don't get complacent
In the Advance with Bf5, be careful about the timing of ...e6 vs ...c5 to avoid blocking your own bishop

variations.name: Classical Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 Nc3 dxe4 Nxe4 Bf5 Ng3 Bg6 h4 h6 Nf3 Nd7 h5 Bh7 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 e6 Bf4 Ngf6 O-O-O Be7 Ne4 O-O
variations.explanation: The Classical is the flagship line of the Caro-Kann, showcasing its key advantage: the light-squared bishop develops to f5 before ...e6, unlike the French where it gets trapped. After the bishop exchange on d3, Black builds a rock-solid structure with ...e6, ...Nd7, ...Ngf6, and ...Be7. White's h4-h5 push gains space but costs tempi, and the h5 pawn can become a long-term weakness. Black completes development and castles into a safe position ready for ...c5 counterplay.
variations.shortExplanation: Classical: ...Bf5 develops before ...e6, bishop trade on d3, then rock-solid setup with ...Be7/O-O.

variations.name: Advance Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 e5 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 c5 Be3 Nd7 O-O Ne7 Nbd2 Nc6 c3 cxd4 cxd4 Qb6
variations.explanation: The Advance Caro-Kann mirrors the French Advance but with a crucial advantage — the bishop is already active on f5 instead of being locked behind the e6 pawn. Black prepares the standard ...c5 break to undermine d4 while developing pieces harmoniously. The knight maneuver ...Ne7-c6 adds pressure to d4, and after ...cxd4 cxd4, the isolated d4 pawn becomes a target for ...Qb6 hitting both d4 and b2.
variations.shortExplanation: Advance with active ...Bf5 (not French-style locked bishop). ...c5/Ne7-c6/Qb6 hits d4 and b2.

variations.name: Two Knights Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 Nf3 d5 Nc3 Bg4 h3 Bxf3 Qxf3 e6 d4 Nf6 Bd3 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Qxe4 Nd7 O-O Nf6 Qh4 Be7 Re1 Nd5
variations.explanation: The Two Knights sidesteps the main Classical tabiya by developing knights before pushing d4. Black responds with active ...Bg4, pinning the f3 knight and forcing h3. After ...Bxf3 Qxf3, Black has traded the light-squared bishop for a knight — a worthy exchange since the bishop has already done its job. The Qh4 swing prepares kingside threats, but Black's ...Nd5 centralization blocks them and Black equalizes with solid piece play.
variations.shortExplanation: Two Knights: ...Bg4 pin, ...Bxf3 trade. Master line ends with Qh4-Re1; Black centralizes ...Nd5.

variations.name: Panov-Botvinnik Attack
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 exd5 cxd5 c4 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Nf3 Bb4 cxd5 Nxd5 Bd2 Nc6 Bd3 O-O O-O Be7 a3 Bf6 Be3 Nce7 Ne4 b6 Qe2 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Panov creates an isolated queen's pawn (IQP) position after c4 and cxd5, leading to a classic structural battle. White gets active pieces and attacking chances thanks to the IQP's support of e5 and c5 outposts, while Black targets the d4 weakness. After ...Bb4 and ...Nxd5, Black establishes a knight on the powerful d5 square blockading the isolani. The ...Nce7-b6-Bb7 setup builds long-diagonal pressure and prepares to trade pieces toward a favorable endgame.
variations.shortExplanation: Panov IQP battle: ...Nxd5 blockade + ...Bf6/Nce7/Bb7 long-diagonal setup; trade pieces to exploit IQP.

variations.name: Fantasy Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 dxe4 fxe4 e5 Nf3 Bg4 Bc4 Nd7 O-O Ngf6 c3 Bd6 Nbd2 O-O Qe1
variations.explanation: The Fantasy Variation is White's aggressive attempt to maintain e4 with f3 rather than defending it with pieces. Black should immediately strike with ...e5 to challenge the center while it is still fluid. The bishop develops actively to g4, pinning the knight and creating tactical pressure. After natural development with ...Bd6, ...O-O, and the Qe1 setup by White, Black stands comfortably — White's f3 has weakened the kingside and Black has clear targets.
variations.shortExplanation: Fantasy (f3): strike with ...e5 immediately; ...Bg4 pin + ...Bd6/O-O keeps White's weakened king under pressure.

variations.name: Short Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 e5 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 Nd7 O-O Ne7 Nbd2 h6 Nb3 Ng6 Ne1 h5 Be3 c5 Nxc5 Bxc5 dxc5 Ngxe5
variations.explanation: Named after Nigel Short, this quiet system avoids the sharp main lines of the Advance. White develops modestly with Be2 and O-O. Black's bishop is well-placed on f5 before ...e6, and the knight reroutes via e7 to g6 targeting e5. The Ne1 maneuver tries to reroute, but Black plays ...h5/c5 with ...Nxc5/dxc5/Ngxe5 — capturing the central pawn after the trades. The resulting position has Black with active pieces and a healthy structure.
variations.shortExplanation: Short Variation: quiet Be2/O-O; Black reroutes ...Ne7-Ng6, then ...c5/Nxc5 captures the e5 pawn.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 exd5 cxd5 Bd3 Nc6 c3 Nf6 Bf4 Bg4 Qb3 Qd7 Nd2 e6 Ngf3 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6 O-O O-O Rfe1 Rab8 Ne5 Bh5 a4 Bg6
variations.explanation: The Exchange Variation without c4 leads to a quieter game than the Panov, but Black should not relax. White aims for a small edge based on the slightly more active minor pieces and the open e-file. Black develops actively with ...Nc6, ...Bg4, and ...Bd6, maintaining equality through piece activity. After the Bxd6 trade, Black's Rab8 prepares ...b5 expansion and the ...Bh5-Bg6 maneuver keeps the bishop active on the long diagonal.
variations.shortExplanation: Exchange (no c4): healthy structure for Black. ...Bg4 pin + ...Bd6 trade + ...Rab8/b5 queenside play.

variations.name: Tartakower/Breyer Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 Nc3 dxe4 Nxe4 Nf6 Nxf6+ exf6 c3 Bd6 Bd3 O-O Qc2 Re8+ Ne2 h5 Be3 Nd7 O-O-O Nf8 Kb1 Be6 c4 Rc8
variations.explanation: Black voluntarily accepts doubled f-pawns after Nxf6+ exf6, gaining the half-open e-file and the bishop pair in return. The doubled pawns are not as weak as they appear — the f5 square becomes available for pieces, and the f6 pawn supports a future ...f5-f4 kingside advance. After opposite-side castling, ...Be6 develops the bad bishop and ...Rc8 prepares queenside operations against White's king on b1.
variations.shortExplanation: Doubled f-pawns + bishop pair + e-file. After opposite-side castle, ...Be6/Rc8 builds queenside attack.

warningLines.name: Advance Bishop Exchange Danger
warningLines.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 e5 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 Nd7 O-O Be7 Nh4 Bg6 Nxg6 hxg6 c3 c5 Nd2 Ngf6 Nf3 Qb6 dxc5 Nxc5
warningLines.explanation: In the Short/Advance Variation, White's Nh4 exchanges off Black's prized light-squared bishop — the very piece that distinguishes the Caro-Kann from the French. After Nxg6 hxg6, Black gets a half-open h-file but loses the bishop advantage permanently. Black must prevent this by playing ...c5 early before White gets Nh4 in, or by rerouting the bishop to h7 where it remains safe. Losing this bishop turns the position into a worse version of the French Defence.
warningLines.shortExplanation: Nh4 exchanges off the prized light-squared bishop, turning Caro-Kann into a worse French. Prevent with early ...c5 or ...Bh7.

name: Pirc Defence
pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Bd3 Na6 O-O c5 d5 Bg4 Bc4 Nc7 h3 Bxf3 Qxf3 a6 a4 b6 Qd3 Qc8 f5 Nd7 Qg3 Ne8 Qh4 Bf6 Qe1 Bd4+ Kh1 Nef6 Qh4 Qb7 Bh6 Be5 Rae1 Rfb8 fxg6 hxg6 Be2 b5 axb5 axb5 Rf3 c4
overview: The Pirc Defence is named after Slovenian GM Vasja Pirc, who pioneered the 1.e4 d6 + 2.d4 Nf6 + 3.Nc3 g6 setup in the 1940s as part of the hypermodern movement. It's the same hypermodern philosophy as the King's Indian and Grünfeld: LET White build a big pawn centre, then strike to break it down. Boris Spassky played the Pirc in his championship matches; modern champions Topalov and Anand have used it as a surprise weapon. The g7 fianchetto pressures the long diagonal; …Na6-c7 develops with tempo against the d-pawn. After …c5 d5, the …Bg4-Bxf3 trade simplifies White's attack and …a6-b6 prepares …Bb7 undermining d5. White's f5 push targets the king; Black's …b5 expansion cracks the a-file. Patience plus precise counterattacking timing wins.
shortOverview: Hypermodern: let White build the center, strike with ...c5, fianchetto pressure on the long diagonal, ...Bg4-Bxf3 trade simplifies White's attack.
keyIdeas: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure d4 and the long diagonal
Let White overextend in the center, then strike back with ...c5 or ...e5
Castle kingside quickly and play for counterplay on the queenside or center
Use the flexibility of the setup — you can transition into many different structures
traps: After e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7, premature 5.e5 hands Black a strong central knight via ...dxe5 fxe5 Nd5
The ...Qa5 pin on c3 can be annoying for White if the knight is unprotected
warnings: White's Austrian Attack (f4) is very aggressive — you must know the theory or you'll get steamrolled
Don't play too passively or White's space advantage becomes crushing

variations.name: Austrian Attack
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Bd3 Na6 O-O c5 d5 Nc7 a4 Rb8 Qe1 b6 Qh4
variations.explanation: The Austrian Attack with f4 is White's most aggressive system against the Pirc. Black counters with the standard ...c5 and ...Na6-c7 maneuver before White's kingside expansion. The rook lift ...Rb8 and ...b6 prepares the queenside fianchetto with ...Bb7, undermining d5 and supporting later ...b5. After Qe1-h4 White retains kingside ambitions while Black completes development on the queenside.
variations.shortExplanation: White's f4 storm. Black counters with ...c5, ...Na6-c7, then ...Rb8-b6-Bb7 to undermine d5 from the queenside.
keyIdeas: Don't fear the big e4-d4-f4 centre — strike its base with ...c5 before White rolls f4-f5 forward.
Reroute the knight ...Na6-c7 to support the ...b5 break.
When White locks with d5, switch wings: load the b-file with ...Rb8, ...b6, ...Bb7 and break with ...b5.
It's a race — counter on the queenside as fast as White attacks your king; never just sit and defend.

variations.name: Classical System
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O c6 a4 Nbd7 h3 e5 dxe5 dxe5 Be3 Qe7 Qd3 Nh5
variations.explanation: The Classical System with Nf3 and Be2 is White's most solid approach. Black equalizes with ...c6 and ...Nbd7, preparing ...e5 at the right moment to challenge the center. After ...dxe5 dxe5, the open d-file and central pawn structure lead to a balanced position. The follow-up ...Qe7 and ...Nh5 prepare kingside reorganization while keeping the dynamic g7 bishop active.
variations.shortExplanation: Quiet Classical. Black equalizes with ...c6, ...Nbd7, then ...e5 to challenge the center; ...Qe7-Nh5 reorganizes for the middlegame.
keyIdeas: Equalise with the classical scheme: ...c6 and ...Nbd7 to prepare the freeing ...e5 break.
Time ...e5 to challenge d4 head-on; after dxe5 dxe5 the open d-file gives easy play.
Put the queen on e7 to back the e5-pawn and connect the rooks.
Reroute the knight toward the f4 outpost — active pieces are your answer to White's quiet setup.

variations.name: 150 Attack
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Be3 Bg7 Qd2 O-O f3 c6 Bh6 Bxh6 Qxh6 Qa5
variations.explanation: The 150 Attack trades the dark-squared bishops with Bh6 to permanently weaken Black's kingside, preparing queenside castling and an h-pawn storm. Black's defense centers on rapid queenside counterplay starting with ...c6 and the immediate ...Qa5 hitting the c3 knight. The ...Qa5 pin creates concrete threats against White's coordination while preparing ...Nbd7 and ...b5-b4 to undermine the c3 outpost.
variations.shortExplanation: White trades dark-squared bishops via Bh6 for an h-pawn storm; Black answers with ...c6 and ...Qa5 pinning c3 for queenside counterplay.
keyIdeas: Meet the blunt plan with speed: ...c6 launches your ...b5-b4 counter-storm before the h-pawn arrives.
Allow the Bh6 trade — your dark squares get airier, but the counterplay arrives in time.
Strike first with ...Qa5, hitting c3 and backing the queenside pawns.
Castle into the race knowing it's mutual: your b-pawn at his king versus his h-pawn at yours.

variations.name: Byrne Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Bg5 Bg7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O c6 f4 b5 e5 b4 exf6 bxc3 Qxc3 exf6 Bh4 d5 Nf3
variations.explanation: The Byrne Variation features Bg5 and opposite-side castling, creating a razor-sharp mutual attack. Black races with ...c6 and ...b5-b4, sacrificing the f6 knight for queenside structure breakdown. After ...exf6 and the queen recapturing on c3, Black's ...d5 central break opens the position for active piece play. The retreated Bh4 is awkwardly placed while Black consolidates with active central development.
variations.shortExplanation: Razor-sharp Bg5 + opposite-side castling. Black races with ...b5-b4, sacrifices f6, then ...d5 opens the position favorably.
keyIdeas: White castles long — attack the king with ...c6 and ...b5-b4 at full speed.
Be ready to sacrifice the f6-knight to crack open White's queenside cover.
Rip the centre open with ...d5 so the g7-bishop and heavy pieces pour toward c3.
In opposite-side castling, don't defend — race; whoever's storm lands first wins.

variations.name: Lion Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Nf3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O a4 c6 Re1 Qc7 h3 b6 Bg5 Bb7 dxe5 Nxe5
variations.explanation: The Lion Variation avoids the fianchetto, choosing ...e5 to create a Philidor-like structure. Black develops ...Nbd7 and ...Be7 with maximum flexibility, then prepares queenside expansion with ...b6 supporting ...Bb7. The Bg5 pin is answered by ...Bb7 with central control, and ...dxe5 Nxe5 opens the position with an active knight in the center.
variations.shortExplanation: Avoids fianchetto, ...e5 creates a Philidor structure. ...Nbd7-Be7 flexible setup, then ...b6-Bb7 supports central pressure.
keyIdeas: Skip the fianchetto: ...e5 steers into a solid Philidor-like structure, dodging Austrian theory.
Build flexibly with ...Nbd7, ...Be7 and ...c6 — keep every option open.
Fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7 to pressure the long diagonal.
Coil for ...b6 and queenside expansion; centralise a knight on e5 when White trades.

variations.name: Fianchetto System
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 g3 Bg7 Bg2 O-O Nge2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 a4 Nd7 Be3 f5
variations.explanation: The Fianchetto System with g3 and Bg2 is White's quietest approach. Black aims for ...e5 followed by ...f5 to challenge the light squares and seize kingside space. The ...Ne7-f5 knight maneuver after d5 places the knight on the ideal blockading square. ...Nd7 supports a future ...c6 break or ...e4 push to constrict the g2 bishop.
variations.shortExplanation: Quiet g3/Bg2 setup. Black plays ...e5 followed by ...f5 to challenge light squares; the ...Ne7-f5 maneuver is key.
keyIdeas: Against White's quiet fianchetto, play for the centre: ...e5 and ...Nc6 to hit d4.
When White locks with d5, reroute ...Ne7 toward the f5 blockade square.
Win the kingside with the ...f5 break — get there before White's f4.
Use your space to open lines for the g7-bishop and the rooks.

variations.name: Czech Defence
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 c6 f4 Qa5 Bd3 e5 Nf3 Bg4 Be3 Nbd7 O-O Be7 h3 Bxf3 Qxf3 O-O Ne2 c5 dxe5 dxe5
variations.explanation: The Czech Defence with ...c6 avoids the sharp theoretical lines of the Austrian Attack. Black builds a compact structure with ...c6 and ...Qa5, pinning the c3 knight and delaying the fianchetto. The plan is direct: challenge the center with ...e5 rather than undermining from the flanks. After ...Bxf3 and ...c5, the position simplifies to a balanced middlegame with active piece play.
variations.shortExplanation: ...c6 + ...Qa5 setup avoids the sharp Austrian Attack. Black challenges the center directly with ...e5 and the ...Bxf3 trade simplifies.
keyIdeas: Choose the compact ...c6 setup over the fianchetto to sidestep the sharpest Austrian lines.
Pin the c3-knight with ...Qa5 to freeze the centre and prepare ...e5.
Challenge the centre directly with ...e5; pin its defender with ...Bg4.
Trade to loosen White's grip, then strike with ...c5 — aim for a sound, harmonious game.

variations.name: Austrian Attack with e5 c5
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O e5 Nfd7 Be2 c5
variations.explanation: When White pushes e5 early in the Austrian Attack, Black's powerful response is ...c5 breaking down White's center support. The ...Nfd7 retreat (rather than the awkward Ng8 alternative) keeps the knight active. With ...c5 hitting d4, Black creates immediate central pressure before White can consolidate.
variations.shortExplanation: Black answers early e5 with ...Nfd7 retreat and ...c5 break to crack White's overextended center.
keyIdeas: When White lunges e5, retreat the knight to d7 (not the rim) — keep it active and pressing e5.
Hit the base of the chain with ...c5 — an advanced pawn no longer defends what it left behind.
Don't blockade the centre; undermine it — the overextended pawns become targets.
Trust the hypermodern idea: meet brute force with a precise central counter-punch.

name: Scandinavian Defence
pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 Bd2 Bb4 O-O Nc6 a3 Bxc3 Bxc3 Qb6 Re1 O-O-O Qc1 Nd5 Bxd5 Rxd5 h3 h6
overview: The Scandinavian Defence has ancient roots — it appears in chess manuscripts dating to 1475, making it one of the oldest analyzed openings on record. Modern revival came from Danish GM Bent Larsen and English GM Tony Miles, who famously beat Anatoly Karpov with the Scandinavian in 1980 (a stunning upset at the time). It's one of the most straightforward responses to 1.e4 — challenge the centre with …d5 and recapture with the queen. The queen comes out early but after …Qa5 you develop rapidly with …Nf6, …Bf5, …e6 and complete the setup with …Bb4 + Bxc3 disrupting White's pawn structure. Black queenside-castles into an opposite-side attacking middlegame.
shortOverview: Challenge with ...d5, recapture ...Qxd5-Qa5, develop ...Nf6/Bf5/e6/Bb4, then ...Bxc3 wrecks White's queenside pawns.
keyIdeas: Recapture on d5 with the queen and relocate to a5 for active play
Develop the light-squared bishop to f5 BEFORE playing ...e6
Build a solid structure with ...c6, ...e6, ...Nbd7, and castle queenside or kingside
Use the early queen activity to put pressure on White's development
traps: After Qa5, Bd2 Qb6 attacks b2, and if White defends carelessly, Black can win a pawn
The ...Nb4 jump can create tactical threats against c2 and d3 in many lines
warnings: Don't wander with the queen too much — each queen move is a tempo White uses to develop
The ...Qxd5 lines with Nc3 Qa5 d4 require knowing where to put the queen if White plays Bd2

variations.name: Qa5 Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 Bd2 c6 Qe2 Bb4 O-O-O Nbd7 Kb1 O-O
variations.explanation: The main line Scandinavian with Qa5 is the most reliable and well-tested variation. Black develops the bishop to f5 before closing the diagonal with ...e6, then builds a compact structure with ...c6 and ...Nbd7. After White castles queenside the position becomes double-edged with mutual attacks. Black can castle kingside (as here) or queenside depending on White's setup, and the ...Bb4 pin on c3 creates persistent pressure.
variations.shortExplanation: Qa5 mainline: ...Bf5 before ...e6, then ...c6/Nbd7 setup. Black castles after kingside development.

variations.name: Nf6 Modern Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 d4 Nxd5 Nf3 g6 Be2 Bg7 O-O O-O c4 Nb6 Nc3 Nc6 d5 Na5 c5 Bxc3 bxc3 Nxd5 Qd4 Nc6
variations.explanation: The Modern Scandinavian with ...Nf6 avoids the early queen exposure entirely, recapturing with the knight instead. Black fianchettoes the kingside bishop for a Grunfeld-like structure where the g7 bishop pressures d4 from the long diagonal. The knight retreats to b6 then back to ...Nc6-Na5 attacking the c4 pawn. The c5-Bxc3 sequence trades the dark-squared bishop, and ...Nxd5-Nc6 creates active piece play against White's center.
variations.shortExplanation: Modern: ...Nxd5 recapture, fianchetto kingside, ...Nc6-Na5 attacks c4, ...Bxc3 trade gives active counterplay.

variations.name: Icelandic Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 c4 e6 dxe6 Bxe6 d4 Bb4+ Bd2 Qe7 Bxb4 Qxb4+ Qd2 Nc6 d5 O-O-O Nc3 Bg4 f3 Rhe8+ Be2 Bf5
variations.explanation: The Icelandic Gambit is a sharp pawn sacrifice where Black gives up the d5 pawn to gain rapid development and active piece play. After ...e6 forces dxe6 Bxe6, Black has all minor pieces developing while White struggles to complete development. The Bb4+ pin disrupts White's coordination, and Black queenside-castles for fast piece coordination on the d-file and e-file. The bishop pair plus open lines provide lasting compensation for the pawn.
variations.shortExplanation: Sharp pawn sacrifice: ...e6 forces dxe6 Bxe6, then Bb4+ pin + queenside castle = active piece play.

variations.name: Qd6 Tiviakov Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qd6 d4 Nf6 Nf3 a6 Be2 Bf5 O-O e6 Ne5
variations.explanation: The Qd6 variation, popularized by Tiviakov, keeps the queen centrally and avoids the theoretical battles of the Qa5 lines. The piece on d6 supports ...e5 if needed and can swing to b6 or c7 depending on White's setup. Black develops with ...a6 preparing ...b5, and ...Bf5 remains the priority bishop development. The Ne5 jump by White is the critical test of Black's setup.
variations.shortExplanation: Tiviakov ...Qd6 setup. ...a6 prepares ...b5; ...Bf5 + ...e6 + flexible structure; Ne5 is critical test.

variations.name: Portuguese Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 d4 Bg4 Be2 Bxe2 Qxe2 Qxd5 Nf3 e6 O-O Nc6 c4
variations.explanation: The Portuguese Variation features the early ...Bg4 development before recapturing the d5 pawn, exchanging Black's light-squared bishop for White's. This trade eliminates the piece that typically becomes passive behind the ...e6 pawn chain. After the queen recaptures on e2, Black gets a harmonious position with no weaknesses. The c4 push by White is the typical test, after which Black completes development with ...Nc6 and ...Be7.
variations.shortExplanation: Portuguese: ...Bg4-Bxe2 trade eliminates the bad bishop, then ...Qxd5 + ...Nc6 + clear development.

variations.name: Gubinsky-Melts (3...Qd8)
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qd8 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bg4 Bc4 e6 h3 Bh5 O-O
variations.explanation: The Gubinsky-Melts retreat with 3...Qd8 looks passive but avoids all queen harassment lines and allows Black to develop naturally. The queen retreats only once instead of being chased around the board, saving tempo in the long run. Black plays ...Bg4 to pin the f3 knight and ...e6 supports the structure. The h3-Bh5 sequence is forced and Black completes development with ...Nc6 and ...Be7 next.
variations.shortExplanation: Gubinsky-Melts ...Qd8 retreat avoids harassment. ...Bg4 pin + ...e6 + classical development.

variations.name: Qa5 with Bd2 Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bd2 c6 Bc4 e6 Qe2 Bb4 O-O-O Nbd7 Kb1 Qc7 Ne5
variations.explanation: When White plays Bd2 instead of the sharper Bc4 lines, a slower strategic battle develops where both sides maneuver for advantage. Black's key idea is ...Bb4 pinning the c3 knight, and after O-O-O the position becomes a mutual attack. The ...Qc7 covers important central squares while the Ne5 jump by White is the critical test — Black must find precise responses to maintain coordination.
variations.shortExplanation: Bd2 line: ...Bb4 pin, opposite-side castle, ...Qc7 centralizes; Ne5 is White's critical test.

name: Alekhine's Defence
pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb6 Nc3 O-O Be3 N8d7 exd6 cxd6 b3 Nf6 Rc1 Nbd7 h3 Bh5 g4 Bg6 Nh4 d5 Nxg6 hxg6 Bf3 dxc4 bxc4 Rc8 c5 b6
overview: Alekhine's Defence is named after Russian World Champion Alexander Alekhine, who introduced it at the 1921 Budapest tournament — an opening that broke every classical rule by inviting White to push pawns forward. The chess world initially dismissed it as 'unsound provocation' until Alekhine demonstrated its strength repeatedly; modern champions Tal, Korchnoi, and Carlsen have all used it as a surprise weapon. After 1...Nf6 you provoke 2.e5 attacking the knight, then retreat …Nd5 and develop while White commits to a big centre. The Modern mainline goes …Bg4 pin and …e6 with c4 and exd6 simplification; Black's flexibility outweighs White's space. The middlegame is about manoeuvring against White's central pawn duo and queenside expansion with …b6.
shortOverview: Provoke 2.e5 with ...Nf6, retreat ...Nd5, develop while White overextends. The Bg4 pin and ...e6 setup gives flexible counterplay.
keyIdeas: Provoke White's pawns forward to create targets for counterattack
Use the knight's retreat to develop pieces with tempo against the extended center
Strike back with ...d6 and ...c5 to undermine the pawn chain
Aim for dynamic piece play once White's center starts to crumble
traps: After e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6, if White plays carelessly Black recaptures with good development
The pin ...Bg4 against the f3 knight is a recurring tactical theme that pressures d4
warnings: White's Four Pawns Attack is extremely aggressive — precise play is required
Don't let White consolidate a massive center without challenging it

variations.name: Four Pawns Attack
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 f4 dxe5 fxe5 Nc6 Be3 Bf5 Nc3 e6 Nf3 Bb4 Rc1
variations.explanation: White goes all-in with four center pawns, but this overextension creates targets. After ...dxe5 fxe5, Black develops with tempo against the hanging pawns. The ...Nc6 attack on d4 and e5 forces tactical complications, while ...Bf5 and ...Bb4 create concrete pressure on the queenside. Rc1 prepares c5 to fix Black's queenside structure before the position opens.
variations.shortExplanation: White's four-pawn center overextends. Black plays ...dxe5 fxe5 and develops with tempo: ...Nc6, ...Bf5, ...Bb4.

variations.name: Modern Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb6 Nc3 O-O Be3 d5 c5 Bxf3 Bxf3 Nc4 Bf4 b6 b3 Na5
variations.explanation: The Modern Variation with Nf3 and Be2 is the most popular main line. Black develops the bishop to g4 early, pinning the f3 knight before ...e6 traps it behind the pawn chain. After ...d5 and the c5 push, the ...Bxf3-Bxf3 trade is followed by ...Nc4 activating the knight on a great outpost. White's Bf4 development and b3 keep the queenside flexible while Black plays for ...b6 and ...Na5 maneuvering.
variations.shortExplanation: Modern Nf3+Be2 setup. ...Bg4 pin, ...e6, then ...Bxf3 trade and ...Nc4-Na5 for queenside counterplay.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6 exd6 Nf3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Nc6 Nc3 Bf5 b3
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d6 leading to a quieter, more strategic game. Black gets easy development and bishop pair potential. The d6 pawn looks isolated but is well-supported by the active piece configuration. The ...Bf5 development pressures e4 while ...Nc6 hits d4, and b3 prepares slow queenside expansion before the middlegame plans crystallize.
variations.shortExplanation: Quiet exchange line. Black develops with ...Be7, ...O-O, ...Nc6, ...Bf5 pressuring the center.

variations.name: Two Pawns Attack
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 c4 Nb6 d4 d6 exd6 exd6 Nf3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Bf5 Nc3 Nc6 Be3 d5 c5 Nc4 Bxc4 dxc4 Qa4
variations.explanation: A more restrained version of the Four Pawns Attack — White builds with c4 and d4 without overextending with f4. Black develops actively against the d4 pawn with ...Bf5 and ...Nc6. The ...d5 break followed by ...Nc4 exchanges pieces and isolates the c-pawn after Bxc4. Qa4 attacks the queenside while Black has the bishop pair to compensate.
variations.shortExplanation: Restrained two-pawn center. ...Bf5-Nc6 hits d4, then ...d5-Nc4 break wins the bishop pair.

variations.name: Chase Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 c4 Nb6 c5 Nd5 d4 d6 cxd6 exd6 Nf3 dxe5 dxe5 Bb4+ Bd2 O-O Bc4 Nc6 O-O Bg4
variations.explanation: The Chase Variation features the aggressive c4-c5 push driving Black's knight back to d5. After ...d6, White's cxd6 simplifies the position. Black takes on e5 with ...dxe5 dxe5, then plays ...Bb4+ to disrupt White's coordination. The ...Bg4 pin completes development with active piece play in an open position where Black's better-coordinated minor pieces compensate for the early knight chase.
variations.shortExplanation: c4-c5 chase. After ...Nd5 d4 d6 cxd6, Black plays ...dxe5 and ...Bb4+ for active piece play.

variations.name: Scandinavian Transposition
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 dxe5 Nxe5 Nd7 Nxd7 Bxd7 c4
variations.explanation: Black transposes into a Scandinavian-flavored structure by exchanging on e5 early. The ...Nd7-Bxd7 simplifies the position with reduced tension. White's c4 expands but the structure is balanced, and Black's bishops have clear development paths via ...Nc6 and ...g6-Bg7. The trade-heavy approach suits players who want a quieter Alekhine middlegame.
variations.shortExplanation: Exchange on e5 early via ...dxe5 Nxe5 Nd7 — Scandinavian-flavored simplification with balanced piece play.

variations.name: Voronezh Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6 cxd6 Nc3 g6 Be3 Bg7 Rc1 O-O b3 e5 d5 h6 Nf3 Bg4
variations.explanation: The Voronezh Variation with c4 and exd6 cxd6 leads to a complex strategic battle. Black fianchettoes and plays for ...e5 to establish a strong central foothold while White expands on the queenside. After ...e5 d5, the position closes with both sides preparing the long maneuvering middlegame. The ...h6-Bg4 development supports kingside flexibility against White's eventual Nf3 setup.
variations.shortExplanation: c4+exd6 cxd6 → Black fianchettoes, plays ...e5, then ...h6-Bg4 for the maneuvering middlegame.

name: Philidor Defence
pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 b6 Be3 Bb7 Qd2 d5
overview: The Philidor Defence is named after François-André Danican Philidor, the legendary 18th-century French master who was the strongest player in the world and famously declared 'pawns are the soul of chess' in his 1749 treatise *Analyse du Jeu des Échecs*. The Philidor was the principal Black defence to 1.e4 for over a century before being eclipsed by the Italian and Spanish. Today it's an underrated, solid choice — instead of the common …Nc6, you play …d6 supporting the e5-pawn and giving a flexible setup. You develop with …Nf6, …Be7, …O-O, and …Nbd7, creating a compact structure. The Hanham setup in particular is a fortress even GMs struggle to break down.
keyIdeas: Support the e5 pawn with ...d6 to create a solid central structure
Use the Hanham setup: ...Nf6, ...Be7, ...O-O, ...Nbd7, ...c6 for maximum solidity
Break with ...d5 when the timing is right to equalize
The knight on d7 can reroute to f8-g6 or b6-c4 depending on the position
traps: After d4 exd4 Nxd4, ...Nf6 e5 looks scary but ...dxe5 is fine
The ...Be7 and ...O-O development avoids many tactical tricks that plague other lines
warnings: Don't play ...exd4 too early or White gets a strong center and open lines for free
The position can become cramped — always have a plan to play ...d5 eventually

variations.name: Hanham Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 a5 d5 Nc5 Bg5 Qc7 Bxf6 Bxf6 Nd2 Be7 Nb3 Nxb3
variations.explanation: The Hanham Variation is the fortress setup that makes the Philidor so reliable. Black builds with ...Nf6, ...Nbd7, ...Be7, ...O-O, and ...c6, creating an impregnable structure that even strong players struggle to crack. The piece on d7 can reroute to c5 targeting e4 and a4, while ...c6 prepares the eventual ...d5 break. White's pieces look impressive but lack concrete targets, and Black's solid wall frustrates aggressive intentions.

variations.name: Nimzowitsch Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Qe2 c6 Rd1 Qc7 a4 b6 dxe5 dxe5 Bg5 Bb7 Nh4 Nc5 Nf5 Rfe8 Nxe7+ Rxe7 Rd2 Rad8
variations.explanation: In the Nimzowitsch setup, White plays Qe2 and Rd1 to control the d-file, aiming for a positional squeeze. Black responds with ...c6, ...Qc7, and ...b6 to develop the bishop to b7 where it supports the central ...d5 break. After White exchanges on e5, the position opens slightly but Black's pieces are well-coordinated. The light-squared bishop on b7 becomes a powerful piece once the center is contested, and ...Nc5 targets both e4 and a4.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Nbd7 f4 Nc5 Bf3 Re8 Kh1 a5 Be3 a4 a3 Bf8 Qd2 c6 Rad1 Qa5 Rfe1 Bd7
variations.explanation: White captures on d4 leading to a more open game where piece activity matters more than structure. Black develops smoothly with ...Nf6, ...Be7, and ...O-O, then reroutes the knight to c5 where it pressures e4 and can jump to e6 or d3. The ...Re8 move increases central pressure and ...a5-a4 fixes White's queenside. Black's position is flexible and easy to play, with clear plans involving the ...c6 and ...d5 break when the timing is right.

variations.name: Antoshin Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 dxe5 Nxe4 Bd3 d5 Nbd2 Nxd2 Bxd2 Be7 O-O O-O c4 Nd7 cxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxd5 Nf3 Bd6 Bc3 Bf5 Bxf5 Qxf5 Qd4 Rfe8 Rfe1 c6
variations.explanation: The Antoshin Variation arises after dxe5 Nxe4, where Black temporarily wins a pawn but must handle the resulting complications accurately. The key idea is that after ...d5, Black establishes a strong central presence and the knight captures on d2 disrupting White's coordination. The ensuing endgame-type middlegame is comfortable for Black with the active piece on d5 and the bishops controlling key diagonals. Black should aim for simplification to exploit the extra central space.

variations.name: Lion Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O a4 c6 Re1 b6 Ba2 Bb7 Bg5 h6 Bh4 Qc7 d5 c5 Nd2 Nh7 Bxe7
variations.explanation: The Lion setup adds ...b6 and ...Bb7 to the Hanham formation, giving the light-squared bishop scope along the a8-h1 diagonal. This fianchetto approach is very flexible and creates additional pressure on e4 and d5. The bishop on b7 supports the eventual ...d5 break and controls key central squares. After White plays d5, Black shifts to kingside counterplay with ...Nh7 and ...Ng5, targeting the weakened f3 and h3 squares while maintaining queenside solidity.

variations.name: Philidor Counter-Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 f5 Nc3 fxe4 Nxe4 d5 Neg5 exd4 Nxd4 Bc5 Be3 Bxd4 Bxd4 Nf6 Be2 O-O O-O Nc6
variations.explanation: The Philidor Counter-Gambit with ...f5 is a sharp and ambitious choice that immediately contests the center. After exf4 Nxe4, the position opens dramatically and tactical skill becomes paramount. Black must play precisely after Neg5, but the ...Bc5 development hits d4 and f2 with tempo. The pawn sacrifice leads to active piece play where Black's lead in development and open f-file provide full compensation. This is an excellent weapon for players who enjoy tactical complications.

variations.name: Modern d3 Hybrid
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d3 Nf6 g3 Be7 Bg2 O-O O-O Nc6 Nbd2 Be6 Re1 Qd7 a4 a5 Nc4 Bxc4 dxc4 Nd4 Nxd4 exd4 f4 c5 e5 dxe5 fxe5
variations.explanation: The Modern d3 hybrid creates a King's Indian Attack-like structure where White plays d3, g3, Bg2 instead of the traditional d4. Black develops naturally with ...Nf6, ...Be7, ...O-O, and ...Nc6, maintaining a solid position. The piece on e6 is well-placed supporting the center and can go to c4 after Nc4. After the center opens with e5 and f4, Black's centralized pieces and the passed d4 pawn create lasting counterplay against White's kingside ambitions.

trapLines.name: Legal's Mate Reversal
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 Bc4 Bg4 Nc3 Nf6 h3 Bh5 Nxe5 Bxd1 Bxf7+ Ke7 Nd5+
trapLines.explanation: White tries the famous Legal's Mate trick with Nxe5, sacrificing the queen for a kingside attack. However, if Black knows the defensive path with ...Ke7, the king can escape the mating net by running up the board. After ...Kd6 and ...Kc5, the king reaches safety while Black has won White's queen for only a bishop. This reversal turns White's aggressive idea into a catastrophic material loss.

trapLines.name: d4 Capture Refutation
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 Ng5 O-O Bxf7+ Rxf7 Ne6 Qe8 Nxc7 Qd8 Nxa8 b6 dxe5 dxe5 O-O Bb7 Nd5 Nxd5 exd5 Nc5 Qf3 Nd3 Re1 Nxc1
trapLines.explanation: White tries the piece sacrifice with Bxf7+ and Ne6, a classic attacking motif. But in the Hanham setup, Black is well-prepared with ...Qe8 trapping the knight after it goes to c7. White wins the exchange on a8 but Black's compensation is enormous with the bishop pair, central control, and active knights. The knight eventually reaches d3 via c5, dominating the position and recovering material with interest.

warningLines.name: Cramped Position Danger
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 h6 b3 Re8 Bb2 Bf8 Qd2 Qc7 Rad1 a5 d5 c5 Nh4 Nb6 Bb5 Bd7 Bxd7 Qxd7 Nf5 Kh7
warningLines.explanation: If Black plays too passively without a plan for ...d5, the position becomes very cramped. White methodically improves piece placement with Re1, Bb2, Qd2, and Rad1, building pressure everywhere. The Nf5 jump is the culmination of White's plan, landing the knight on the dream square where it dominates the position. Black must always be planning the ...d5 break or risk being slowly squeezed to death by White's superior space and piece coordination.

name: Petrov Defence
pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6 Bf4 Na5
overview: The Petrov Defence (also called the Russian Game) is named after Russian master Alexander Petrov (or Petroff in transliteration), who analyzed it in the 1840s. It's been Black's drawing weapon for 180 years; modern revival came from Vladimir Kramnik who used it as his main weapon at the elite level in the 1990s — scoring a famous string of Petroff draws and wins against Kasparov. Caruana has played it as his 'absolutely must not lose' opening in critical match situations. By mirroring White's move with 2...Nf6, you challenge e4 and signal you're not afraid of a fight. The positions are solid; the theory leads to lines where Black is never worse.
keyIdeas: Mirror White's attack on e5 with ...Nf6 attacking e4
After Nxe5, play ...d6 first (NOT ...Nxe4 immediately) to force the knight back
Build a solid center with ...d5 and develop smoothly
Use the solid symmetric structure to equalize first, then slowly outplay your opponent in the middlegame
traps: After Nxe5, if White plays Nxf7?? Qe7 wins the knight — there's no safe square
The ...Nc6 development can lead to tactics against d4 if White isn't careful
warnings: Never play 2...Nxe4 3.Nxe5?? — this is wrong! The correct order is 3...d6 4.Nf3 Nxe4
The Cochrane Gambit (Nxf7) is surprisingly dangerous — study it specifically

variations.name: Classical Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6 Re1 Re8 Bf4 Rc8 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 Na5
variations.explanation: The Classical Variation is the theoretical main line where both sides develop naturally after the pawn exchange. Black achieves a symmetric pawn structure with ...d5 and develops the bishop to e7 and f5 for a rock-solid position. The ...Nb4 jump creates pressure on c2 and d3, and after a3, the knight retreats to c6 to maintain central control. The resulting positions are extremely solid for Black, with equality achievable through precise play and the long-term plan of ...Na5-c4 exploiting weak queenside squares.

variations.name: Steinitz Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 Bd3 d5 Nxe5 Nd7 Nxd7 Bxd7 O-O Bd6 c4 c6 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 O-O Qh5 g6 Qh6 f6 Rb1 Bc7 Re1
variations.explanation: The Steinitz Variation with 3.d4 is an older but still fully playable line where White immediately opens the center. Black captures on e4 and builds a solid center with ...d5, then exchanges knights with ...Nd7 to simplify. The ...Bd6 development is natural and after ...O-O, Black's position is compact and hard to break down. White's Qh5 looks aggressive but after ...g6, the queen must retreat or trade, and the resulting positions are balanced with clear plans for both sides.

variations.name: Three Knights Game
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Bb4 Nxc6 bxc6 Bd3 d5 exd5 cxd5 O-O O-O Bg5 Be6 Qf3 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 Qxd5 Qxd5 Nxd5 Bxb2 Rab1 Bf6 Nxf6+ gxf6
variations.explanation: The Three Knights Game arises when White plays Nc3 instead of Nxe5, transposing into a symmetrical position. After d4 exd4 Nxd4, Black plays ...Bb4 pinning the knight and ...bxc6 creates doubled pawns but opens the b-file for counterplay. The position simplifies into an endgame where Black's bishop pair compensates for the doubled pawns. The ...d5 break equalizes fully and Black's active piece play prevents White from exploiting the structural weakness.

variations.name: Cochrane Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nxf7 Kxf7 d4 Be7 Nc3 Re8 Bc4+ Be6 Bxe6+
variations.explanation: The Cochrane Gambit with Nxf7 is a wild piece sacrifice that gives White three pawns and a dangerous initiative. Black must accept with ...Kxf7 and then consolidate carefully with ...Be7 and ...Re8, getting the king to safety. The extra piece should eventually dominate but Black must survive the tactical storm first. The key defensive setup is ...Be6 blocking the check, then ...Rf6 to consolidate, and ...Kg8 to finally castle by hand with a solid extra piece advantage.

variations.name: Italian Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 Nc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Be7 Be3 Nc6 Qd2 Be6 O-O-O Qd7 Kb1 O-O-O Bd3 d5 Nd4 Nxd4 Bxd4 Bf6 Bxf6 gxf6 f4 Kb8 Rhe1 Rhe8
variations.explanation: The Italian Variation features dxc3 recapture giving White the bishop pair and open d-file in exchange for doubled c-pawns. White castles queenside and builds pressure along the d-file, but Black's solid pawn structure and active pieces provide full equality. The knight exchange on c3 opens the position for Black's bishops, and the ...d5 break is the key equalizing move. Black can also castle queenside for a symmetrical battle where the bishop pair is a long-term asset.

variations.name: 5.Bd3 Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Bd6 O-O O-O c4 c6 Qc2 Na6 a3 Bg4 Nbd2 Nxd2 Bxd2 Bxf3 gxf3 Qh4 Be1 Nc7 f4 f5 Kh1 Ne6 Rg1 Rf6
variations.explanation: The 5.Bd3 line is White's most natural developing choice, placing the bishop on an active diagonal aimed at the kingside. Black responds with ...Bd6 and ...O-O, creating a solid King's Indian-like structure. After ...c6 and ...Na6, Black prepares to challenge the center with ...Bg4 pinning the f3 knight. The ...Qh4 foray creates real threats against f2 and the weakened kingside after g3. Black maintains equality through active piece play and the threat of ...f5 to open lines against White's king.

variations.name: Kaufmann Attack
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 Nc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Be7 Bf4 Nc6 Qd2 Be6 O-O-O Qd7 Kb1 O-O-O Nd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Bf6 Qa4 Kb8 Bd3 c6 Be3 d5 f4
variations.explanation: The Kaufmann Attack features Nc3 followed by Bf4, aiming for aggressive development with opposite-side castling. White builds pressure along the d-file and aims for a kingside attack, but Black's solid development with ...Be7, ...Be6, and ...O-O-O neutralizes the threats. The knight exchange on d4 simplifies the position, and after ...Bf6, Black's bishop controls the key long diagonal. Both sides castle queenside leading to a complex middlegame where Black's structural solidity provides a safe foundation for counterplay.

trapLines.name: Nxf7 Refutation
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 Nc6 Nxc6 dxc6 d3 Bc5 Be2 Ng4 O-O Qh4 h3 Nxf2 Rxf2 Bxf2+ Kh1
trapLines.explanation: If White plays the rare Nxc6 line instead of the main 3.Nxe5, Black can build a devastating kingside attack. The ...Bc5 and ...Ng4 combination targets f2 with lethal force, and after ...Nxf2 Rxf2 Bxf2+, the king is exposed. The queen invades to e1 with check, and Black's pieces coordinate for a winning attack. White's extra pawn is irrelevant when the king is under constant fire from Black's bishops and queen.

trapLines.name: Wrong Move Order Punishment
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 Nxe4 Qe2 Qe7 Qxe4 d6 d4 dxe5 dxe5 Nc6 Bf4 Qb4+ Nc3 Qxb2
trapLines.explanation: If Black plays the wrong order with 3...Nxe4 instead of 3...d6, White gets Qe2 pinning the knight against the king. However, Black can still salvage the position by heading for the endgame with ...Qe7. After the queen trade and pawn exchanges, the resulting endgame is tricky but playable. The key is not to panic at the Qe2 pin but to trade queens and rely on the structural balance to achieve a drawable position.

trapLines.name: Steinitz d4 Counter
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 Bd3 d5 Nxe5 Nd7 Nxd7 Bxd7 O-O Bd6 c4 c6 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 O-O Qc2 Re8 Re1 Rxe1+
trapLines.explanation: In the Steinitz Variation, Black can counter White's d4 with solid central play. After ...Nxe4 and ...d5, the position is symmetric but Black's bishop on d6 is actively placed. The knight exchange on c3 doubles White's pawns but opens the b-file for Black's counterplay. The resulting endgame is comfortable for Black with the bishop pair and pressure along the c-file. White's doubled c-pawns are a permanent structural weakness that Black can target.

name: Queen's Gambit
pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 Bf4 Bg4
overview: The Queen's Gambit is one of chess's oldest analyzed openings — documented in Spanish manuscripts dating to the 1490s, refined by Wilhelm Steinitz in his championship matches of the 1880s-1890s. Capablanca, Lasker, Alekhine, Botvinnik, Karpov, Kasparov, Kramnik, Anand, and Carlsen — every world champion since Steinitz has played the Queen's Gambit at some point in their career. The Netflix series 'The Queen's Gambit' (2020) brought the opening to a new generation of viewers. It's not really a gambit since you can always win the pawn back; the point is to either gain a central pawn majority or saddle Black with an isolated queen's pawn. This opening has been a cornerstone of championship chess for 140 years.
keyIdeas: Pressure d5 with c4 — if Black captures, recapture and enjoy the central majority
Develop pieces to optimal squares: Nc3, Bg5, Nf3, e3, Bd3
In the Exchange Variation, play for the minority attack with a3, b4, b5
Control the center and restrict Black's counterplay
traps: If Black plays ...dxc4, don't panic — play e3 and Bxc4 to recapture with development
In the Exchange, the minority attack b4-b5 creates a weak pawn on c6 that's hard to defend
warnings: Don't push e4 too early without preparation — it can backfire if the center opens prematurely
The QGD gives Black a solid position — don't expect quick wins, play for long-term pressure

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Qc2 Re8 Nf3 c6 O-O Nf8 Rab1 a5 a3 Ng6 b4 axb4 axb4 Bd6 b5
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d5 early and launches the minority attack with a3, b4, and b5 to undermine Black's queenside pawn chain. The target is the c6 pawn, which becomes backward or isolated after bxc6. White's plan is crystal clear: pile rooks on the b- and c-files and grind down the structural weakness.

variations.name: Classical Mainline
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5
variations.explanation: The tabiya of the Queen's Gambit Declined where White develops Bg5, e3, Nf3, Rc1, Bd3 in the classic Capablanca order. After Black releases tension with ...dxc4, White recaptures with the bishop and enjoys a lead in development. The key is that White's pieces coordinate beautifully with Rc1 already on the open file, and the initiative persists well into the middlegame.

variations.name: Tartakower Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Rc1 Be6 Qa4 c5 Qa3 Rc8 Bb5
variations.explanation: Black plays ...h6 and ...b6 to fianchetto the light-squared bishop, a favorite of Tartakower. White should exchange on d5 to fix the pawn structure, then target the hanging pawns or isolated d-pawn. The Qa4-a3 maneuver pressures both the queenside and the c5 break. White's edge lies in the superior pawn structure and piece activity along the c-file.

variations.name: Queen's Gambit Accepted
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 Qe2 b5 Bb3 Bb7 Rd1 Nbd7 Nc3 Qb8 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Bxd5 Bxd5 Rxd5 Be7
variations.explanation: Black grabs the c4 pawn immediately. White recovers it effortlessly with e3 and Bxc4, gaining a tempo on development. The central pawn majority (d4 + e3 vs Black's e6) gives White a lasting space advantage. The d5 break is the thematic lever that opens the position in White's favor, especially with a rook already on d1.

variations.name: Slav Defence Response
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 e3 e6 Bxc4 Bb4 O-O O-O Qe2 Nbd7 e4 Bg6 Bd3
variations.explanation: Against the Slav with ...c6, White plays a4 immediately to prevent Black from consolidating with ...b5. The e4 push seizes the center and gains space. After e5, the knight on f6 is pushed away and White gets a strong central bind. The a4 move is critical: without it, Black holds the extra pawn comfortably.

variations.name: Semi-Slav Response
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7 O-O a6 e4 c5 d5 e5
variations.explanation: The Semi-Slav with ...c6 and ...e6 is one of the richest structures in chess. White aims for the e4 push to claim the center, and if Black allows d5, the passed pawn becomes a monster. The sharp tactical play after e4 c5 d5 leads to complex middlegames where White's central space advantage and piece activity provide lasting initiative.

variations.name: Catalan Transposition
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 g3 Be7 Bg2 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2
variations.explanation: White fianchettoes with g3 and Bg2 instead of the classical e3/Bd3 setup, transposing into a Catalan structure. The bishop on g2 is an absolute monster on the long diagonal, pressuring b7 and c6. Combined with Qc2, White creates a battery that makes it very hard for Black to develop the queenside comfortably. This hybrid approach combines QGD piece placement with Catalan diagonal power.

variations.name: Anti-QGD: Early Bf4 System
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bf4 Bd6 Bg3 O-O e3 c5
variations.explanation: Instead of the standard Bg5, White develops the bishop to f4 early, avoiding the main theoretical lines of the QGD. This sidesteps the Tartakower and Lasker variations entirely. If Black challenges with ...Bd6, the retreat to g3 maintains the bishop and after c5 dxc5, White enjoys a comfortable IQP position with active pieces and clear attacking prospects against Black's king.

trapLines.name: QGA b5 Overextension
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 e3 b5 a4 c6 axb5 cxb5 Qf3
trapLines.explanation: Black tries to hold the gambit pawn with ...b5?; White's a4 break rips open the queenside and wins material.

warningLines.name: Elephant Trap
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Nbd7 cxd5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Bxd8 Bb4+ Qd2 Bxd2+ Kxd2 Kxd8
warningLines.explanation: White's tempting 6.Nxd5?? loses a piece to 6...Nxd5 7.Bxd8 Bb4+ winning the bishop back with interest.

name: London System
pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 g6 Bf4 Bg7 e3 d6 Be2 O-O O-O Nbd7 h3 b6 c3 Bb7 Nbd2 Ne4 Nxe4 Bxe4
overview: The London System takes its name from the 1922 London tournament where Capablanca and others experimented with it. It stayed a tournament sideline for decades, used by Soviet greats Tigran Petrosian and Mikhail Botvinnik as ultra-solid drawing weapons. Magnus Carlsen's adoption of the London at the elite level in the 2010s shocked the chess world — proving systems-based openings could compete with sharp theoretical mainlines. It's the ultimate low-maintenance opening: develop Bf4, e3, Nf3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3 in almost any order regardless of what Black does. Bf4 is the star piece; from there you launch kingside attacks or play positionally depending on what Black gives you.
keyIdeas: Develop Bf4 early — this is the signature move, get it out before e3 blocks it in
Build a solid pyramid with d4, e3, c3 — hard to break down
Place pieces on their best squares: Nf3, Bd3, Nbd2, O-O, then choose your plan
If Black plays ...Nh5 to trade the bishop, retreat Bg3 to maintain the pair
traps: If Black plays ...Qb6 hitting b2, don't panic — Qb3 or Rb1 handles it easily
After Bg3, if Black plays ...Nh5, the bishop retreats and the knight on h5 is misplaced
warnings: Don't play Bf4 if Black has already played ...Bf5 — the trade favors Black in many lines
Against the King's Indian setup (...g6, ...Bg7), be ready for ...c5 and ...Qb6 pressure

variations.name: London vs Queen's Pawn
variations.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 c5 c3 Nc6 Nbd2 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 Qe7 Ne5 Nd7 f4 f5 Ndf3 Nf6
variations.explanation: The bread-and-butter London against ...d5. White builds the classic d4-e3-c3 pyramid and places the knight on e5, the ideal outpost in this structure. The f4 push reinforces Ne5 and creates kingside attacking chances. With Qb3 pressuring b7, White combines queenside threats with a potential kingside assault through the f-file.

variations.name: London vs King's Indian
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 g6 e3 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Be2 d6 O-O Nbd7 h3 b6 c3 Bb7 Nbd2 Ne8
variations.explanation: Against the King's Indian fianchetto, White develops with Be2 instead of Bd3 since Black hasn't committed to a piece on e6. The h3 move prevents ...Ng4 tricks and Bh2 preserves the London bishop from exchange attempts. White reroutes the knight via Nbd2-c4 to pressure d6, then strikes with e4 to open the center when Black's knight has retreated.

variations.name: Jobava London
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 d5 Nc3 e6 e3 c5 Nf3 a6 Be2 Nc6 O-O Bd6
variations.explanation: The Jobava London uses Nc3 instead of c3, creating a fundamentally more aggressive setup with immediate ideas of Nb5 or e4. The knight jumps to b5 to harass the queen or threaten Nd6+. After the central break with e4, White seizes space and opens lines for the bishops. This variation has surged in popularity because it gives White active piece play while maintaining the London's solid foundation.

variations.name: London vs Grunfeld Setup
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 g6 e3 Bg7 Nf3 d5 Be2 O-O O-O c5 c3 Nc6 Nbd2 Nd7
variations.explanation: Against Black's Grunfeld-style setup with ...g6 and ...d5, White completes development before choosing a plan. The Nh4 maneuver eyes the f5 square, a powerful outpost in this pawn structure. After Black's ...e5 break, the central exchanges lead to a position where White's bishop pair and better pawn structure give a lasting advantage. The Qb3 move creates pressure on b7 and d5 simultaneously.

variations.name: London vs Dutch
variations.pgn: d4 f5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 d5 Bd3 Bd6
variations.explanation: The London is a superb weapon against the Dutch because Bf4 directly contests the dark squares that Black weakened with ...f5. The bishop trade on d6 saddles Black with a potentially weak e6 pawn and the f5 square becomes a permanent hole. After c4 challenges the center, White opens the f-file and exploits Black's chronically weakened kingside light squares.

variations.name: London vs Bf5 Mirror
variations.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Bf5 e3 e6 Nf3 Nf6 c4 c6 Nc3 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 Nbd7 O-O O-O e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Qxe4 Nf6 Qe2
variations.explanation: When Black mirrors with ...Bf5, White should not shy away from the bishop trade. Instead, play c4 and Nc3 to build a big center, then exchange bishops on d3 and d6. The resulting position after e4 gives White a powerful central pawn majority and open lines. White's superior center and active rooks on the e- and d-files provide a clear positional advantage.

variations.name: London: Nh4-f5 Knight Maneuver
variations.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nd2 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ngf3 Qe7 Ne5 Nd7 Nxc6 bxc6 Nf3
variations.explanation: This sophisticated regrouping maneuver sends the knight to h4 and then f5, where it becomes a dominating piece pressuring e7, d6, and g7. The idea works especially well when Black has committed to ...e6, since the f5 square is permanently weakened. Combined with the battery of Qc2 and Bd3 aimed at the kingside, this creates serious attacking prospects.

variations.name: London vs Early c5 Challenge
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 c5 d5 b5 f3 g6 e4 Bg7 Be3 O-O Bxc5 d6
variations.explanation: When Black plays an early ...c5 challenging the d4 pawn, White advances d5 to seize space. Black's Benko-style gambit with ...b5 looks aggressive but White's solid structure and extra central space are more important. After e3 and c4, White undermines Black's queenside expansion and the d5 pawn becomes a powerful space-gaining wedge dividing Black's army.

name: Catalan Opening
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Nc6 Nc3
overview: The Catalan was christened at the 1929 Barcelona tournament when Savielly Tartakower, asked by the organizers for a new variation, fianchettoed his king's bishop and called it 'the Catalan' in honour of the host region. Botvinnik used it sparingly; it became a true champion's weapon when Vladimir Kramnik adopted it in the 2000s, and Magnus Carlsen made it his signature 1.d4 weapon — winning World Championship games from positions Stockfish calls equal. It combines the Queen's Gambit pawn structure with a fianchettoed bishop on g2 — a monster on the long diagonal exerting pressure from g2 all the way to a8. Positions look quiet but are deceptively dangerous; nearly every top player has this in their repertoire.
keyIdeas: The fianchettoed bishop on g2 creates long-term pressure on the queenside
If Black takes ...dxc4, White often plays Qc2 and recovers the pawn with positional compensation
White has a persistent space advantage and better piece coordination
The bishop on g2 combines with Qc2 to create powerful pressure on c6 and the long diagonal
traps: After ...dxc4, the bishop on g2 rakes down the long diagonal hitting the rook on a8
If Black tries to hold the c4 pawn with ...b5, a4 undermines it and wins the pawn back with interest
warnings: Don't rush to recapture on c4 — the positional pressure is often more valuable than the pawn
Against the Closed Catalan (...c6 without ...dxc4), be patient and avoid premature pawn breaks

variations.name: Open Catalan: dxc4 Qc2 Recovery
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Be7 O-O O-O Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Open Catalan main line where Black captures on c4 and White recovers with Qc2-Qxc4. The bishop on g2 immediately activates on the long diagonal, pressuring b7 and a8. After White recovers the pawn, the positional compensation is enormous — the bishop pair, better pawn structure, and natural piece coordination give White a lasting edge. Black's ...Be4 maneuver tries to block the diagonal, but after Qc1 and Ba5, White repositions smoothly while maintaining pressure on Black's queenside.

variations.name: Closed Catalan: ...c6 System
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8
variations.explanation: In the Closed Catalan, Black keeps the tension with ...c6 and avoids capturing on c4. White's plan revolves around the central e4 push to seize space, after which the bishop on g2 gains power as the center opens. The e5 advance cramps Black severely, forcing the knight to e8 and making development awkward. White's knight can reroute to f5 via d4, creating threats against e7 and g7. The f4 push supports the center and prepares to open lines on the kingside where Black's pieces are congested.

variations.name: Open Catalan: a6-b5 with a4 Break
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O b5 Ne5 Nd5 a4 Bb7 axb5 axb5 Rxa8 Bxa8
variations.explanation: Black tries to hold the c4 pawn with ...a6 and ...b5, which is the most ambitious defensive try. White's a4 break is the critical response — it undermines the b5 pawn and opens the a-file where White already has the rook. After Rxa8 Bxa8, White dominates the a-file and the Ne5 is powerfully placed targeting c4, c6, and d7. The exchange of light-squared bishops via ...Bxg2 Kxg2 actually helps White, as the king is active in the resulting endgame and Black's extra pawn on c4 is easily recovered.

variations.name: Catalan with ...Nbd7 Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7 Nc3 Nb6 Qd3 Nbd5 Nxd5
variations.explanation: Black develops the knight to d7 and aims for a solid setup with ...Nb6 challenging the c4 queen. White plays a4 to prevent ...b5 expansion and recaptures on c4 with a space advantage. The Bf4 development is flexible, supporting e5 and controlling the dark squares. After ...Nbd5, White maintains pressure with Nxd5 Nxd5 and the bishop maneuver Be5-d4, controlling the long dark-square diagonal and restricting Black's piece activity. White's space advantage and better-coordinated pieces provide lasting pressure.

variations.name: Catalan vs Slav Setup: ...c6 and ...dxc4
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 g3 dxc4 Bg2 b5 a4 Bb7 Ne5 a6 axb5
variations.explanation: Against the Slav move order with ...c6, the Catalan structure still works beautifully. After ...dxc4 and ...b5, White plays a4 to undermine Black's queenside pawns and b3 to recover the c4 pawn. The Ne5 placement pressures c6 and d7 while the bishop on g2 rakes down the long diagonal toward a8. After the pawn exchanges, White emerges with superior development, open lines on the a- and b-files, and the powerful Bg2 targeting Black's weakened queenside. The Nc3 and Rd1 setup creates a harmonious piece configuration.

variations.name: Catalan Declined: ...Be7 without ...dxc4
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 b3 b6 Bb2 Bb7 Nc3 Rc8 e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Qxe4
variations.explanation: In the Catalan Declined, Black keeps the center closed with ...c6 and ...Be7, avoiding the ...dxc4 capture entirely. White builds patiently with b3, Bb2, and Nc3, preparing the e4 central break. The double fianchetto with Bb2 and Bg2 creates a powerful crossfire of bishops, and the e4 advance opens the center when White's pieces are perfectly placed. After dxe4 Nxe4, White has a strong centralized knight and open lines for both bishops. Black's solid but passive setup struggles to generate counterplay against White's harmonious piece coordination.

variations.name: Early Qa4+ Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Bd7
variations.explanation: The Qa4+ line is an aggressive early attempt to recover the c4 pawn with check, disrupting Black's development. After ...Bc6 Qxc4, White has the pawn back and threatens Qxc5. Black must play accurately with ...Bd5 to challenge the queen and develop pieces. The resulting positions feature an early queen exchange after ...cxd4 and Qxd4, leading to a nuanced endgame where White's bishop pair and active pieces compensate for Black's solid structure. The Nb5 jump creates threats on c7 and d6 that force Black to solve concrete problems early.

name: Trompowsky Attack
pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 c5 Bd3 Nf6 c3 Nc6 Nd2 Bg4 Ngf3 e6 O-O Rc8 Qa4 Nd7 Ne5 Ndxe5 Bxe5 Qd7 Bg3 Bh5 dxc5 Bxc5 Qh4 Bg6 Bxg6 fxg6 Nf3 Be7 Qf4 Bf6
overview: The Trompowsky Attack is named after Brazilian champion Octavio Trompowsky de Almeida who championed the 2.Bg5 system at international tournaments in the 1930s-40s. It stayed an obscure sideline for decades until English GM Julian Hodgson made it his lifework in the 1980s-90s, winning hundreds of tournament games and proving its theoretical viability. Modern players from Carlsen to Nakamura use it as a surprise weapon to sidestep all of Black's prepared Indian defences. By playing 2.Bg5 immediately, you force Black to deal with the pin on the f6-knight before they can set up their favourite system. It's a practical weapon that gets opponents out of their comfort zone from move two — and the positions are surprisingly rich.
keyIdeas: 2.Bg5 forces Black to react immediately, preventing standard Indian setups
After 2...Ne4 3.Bf4, White gets the bishop pair and a space advantage
The pawn structure often resembles a reversed London with more attacking chances
White can play for e3-e4 or c4 breaks depending on Black's response
traps: After 2...Ne4 3.Bh4? g5! 4.Bg3 Nxg3 5.hxg3, Black has won the bishop pair and damaged White's structure — so play 3.Bf4! instead.
If Black plays 2...d5 3.Bxf6 exf6, White gets a pleasant position with e3 and Bd3, while Black's doubled f-pawns are a permanent weakness.
warnings: Don't play Bg5 and then retreat to h4 on ...Ne4 — always go to f4 to keep the bishop active
Against 2...e6, be ready for a French-type structure — don't overextend in the center

variations.name: 2...Ne4 3.Bf4 Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 c5 Bd3 Nc6
variations.explanation: The most important line in the Trompowsky. After 2...Ne4 3.Bf4, White retreats to the ideal diagonal and maintains the bishop pair. Black returns the knight to f6 and develops with ...Bg4, ...e6, and ...Bd6. White builds a solid center with c3, Nbd2, and aims for the e4 break. The exchange on d6 gives White a pleasant middlegame with better pawn structure and the e4 lever as the primary source of initiative.

variations.name: 2...d5 3.Bxf6 Doubled Pawns
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 d5 Bxf6 exf6 e3 Bd6 Bd3 O-O Nd2 Re8
variations.explanation: White takes on f6 immediately, inflicting permanent doubled pawns on Black. The trade-off is that Black gains the bishop pair and the semi-open e-file. White plays a solid setup with e3, Bd3, Nd2, and Ne2, aiming to exploit the f-pawns in the endgame. The Nf4 outpost is particularly strong, pressuring d5 and eyeing g6. Black must keep the position dynamic and avoid simplifications where the pawn weakness tells.

variations.name: 2...e6 French-Type Structure
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 e6 e4 h6 Bxf6 Qxf6 Nc3 d6 Qd2 Nd7 O-O-O
variations.explanation: Against 2...e6, White transposes into an aggressive system with e4, creating a French Defense structure where the dark-squared bishop has already been exchanged on f6. After h6 and Bxf6 Qxf6, White has a strong center and can castle queenside for a direct kingside attack. The f4-f5 or e5 push creates serious pressure, and Black must be careful not to become passive. White's plan involves Nf3, Bd3, and Qf4 with a powerful initiative.

variations.name: Raptor Variation 2...c5
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 c5 d5 Qb6 Nc3 Qxb2 Bd2 Qb6 e4 d6 f4 e6 Bc4 exd5
variations.explanation: The Raptor Variation is one of the sharpest lines in the Trompowsky. After 2...c5 3.d5 Qb6, Black grabs the b2 pawn, but White gets a massive center with e4 and f4 plus rapid development. The Bd2 retreat prepares Bc4 targeting f7, and after O-O White has a huge lead in development. Black must return material or suffer a devastating attack. The key is that White's center pawns and piece activity provide more than enough compensation for the pawn.

variations.name: 2...g6 Fianchetto Response
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 g6 Bxf6 exf6 c4 d5 cxd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 e3 Bg7
variations.explanation: When Black fianchettoes with 2...g6, White takes on f6 immediately to saddle Black with doubled f-pawns. After exf6, Black has the bishop pair and open e-file but the pawn structure is compromised. White develops with Nc3, e3, Bd3, and Nge2, aiming for the Ng3-e4 maneuver to exploit the weakened kingside. The piece on e4 is a monster, hitting f6 and d6 simultaneously, and Black must work hard to prove the bishops compensate for the structural damage.

variations.name: Vaganian Gambit 2...Ne4 3.Bf4 c5
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 c5 d5 Qb6 Bc1 e6 f3 Nf6 e4 exd5
variations.explanation: The Vaganian Gambit arises when Black plays 3...c5 instead of 3...d5 in the Ne4 Bf4 line. After 4.d5 Qb6, White offers the b2 pawn to accelerate development. If Black accepts with Qxb2, White gets rapid piece play with Nd2, e4, and quick development. The open b-file and central pawn wedge on d5 give White long-lasting compensation. Black must be very precise to hold the extra pawn while fending off White's initiative.

variations.name: 2...Ne4 3.Bf4 c5 4.f3 Sideline
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 c5 f3 Nf6 d5 Qb6 Bc1 d6 e4 g6 Na3
variations.explanation: The 4.f3 line is a sharp alternative where White chases the knight back and builds a powerful center with e4. After Nf6 retreats, White plays d5 clamping down on the center and develops with Na3-c4 targeting d6 and b6. The position resembles a Benoni-type structure where White has extra time from the knight retreat. Black must counterattack on the queenside with ...Qb6, ...a6, and eventually ...b5, or risk being squeezed by White's space advantage.

variations.name: 2...d5 3.Bxf6 gxf6 Recapture
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 d5 Bxf6 gxf6 e3 c5 dxc5 e6 c4 dxc4 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Bxc4 Bxc5 Nf3 Ke7 Nc3 Nd7 O-O
variations.explanation: The gxf6 recapture is the ambitious alternative to exf6. Black accepts a shattered kingside in exchange for the half-open g-file and active piece play. After c5 and e6, Black develops quickly and the position often simplifies into an endgame where Black's bishop pair and active rooks compensate for the damaged structure. White should aim for an early queen exchange with Qxd8+ to exploit the king's inability to castle, but Black's active pieces make this harder than it looks.

name: Queen's Gambit Declined
pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 Bb3 exd4 exd4 Nf6 Re1 Qd6 Ne5 Be6 Bxe6 fxe6 Qb3 Rab8
overview: The Queen's Gambit Declined dates back to chess's earliest analyzed positions — documented in Spanish manuscripts of the 1490s. Siegbert Tarrasch (the 'Praeceptor Germaniae' or Germany's chess teacher) built his teaching career around the QGD; José Raúl Capablanca made it his lifelong defence and never lost a QGD game with Black across his championship career; Anatoly Karpov played the QGD throughout his 1975-1985 World Championship reign without losing a single QGD game with Black. By playing …e6, you support your d5-pawn and prepare to develop naturally. The positions are strategic and rich — you're playing the same lines World Champions have trusted for 150 years.
keyIdeas: Support d5 with ...e6, then develop pieces to natural squares
The ...dxc4 break at the right moment frees Black's position
After ...Nd5, Black gets active piece play and challenges the Bg5
The minority attack with ...b5-b4 on the queenside is a key strategic plan
traps: Elephant Trap: After Bg5 Nbd7, if White plays cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7, Black can spring ...Nxc3! winning the queen if White recaptures bxc3 because of ...Qa3+.
After dxc4 Bxc4, Black plays ...Nd5 hitting the Bg5 and gaining tempo to reorganize.
warnings: Don't play ...dxc4 too early — wait until you can follow up with ...c5 or ...Nd5
Be careful about the e6 pawn becoming a weakness in the endgame — keep the position dynamic

variations.name: Orthodox Defense Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5
variations.explanation: The most classical continuation of the QGD, played by Capablanca and Karpov. Black develops solidly with Be7, castles, and plays Nbd7 before timing the ...dxc4 break perfectly. After ...Nd5 challenges the bishop and ...e5 opens the center, Black achieves full equality with active pieces. The key is understanding that ...dxc4 and ...Nd5 must come together as a package — the piece on d5 is the cornerstone of Black's counterplay.

variations.name: Tartakower Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Rc1 Be6 Qa4 c5 Qa3 Rc8 Bb5 a6 dxc5 bxc5
variations.explanation: Karpov's beloved system where Black plays ...h6 and ...b6, developing the light-squared bishop actively. After the exchange on d5, Black gets the bishop pair and pressure along the c-file and long diagonal. The ...Bb7 plan gives Black excellent control of the light squares, and the ...c5 break challenges White's center. This variation is extremely solid and has been used successfully at the highest level for over a century.

variations.name: Lasker Defense
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 Ne4 Bxe7 Qxe7 cxd5 Nxc3 bxc3 exd5 Qb3 Qd6 c4 dxc4 Bxc4
variations.explanation: Lasker's brilliant simplifying idea plays ...Ne4 early, forcing exchanges that relieve Black's cramped position. After ...Nxc3 and bxc3, White has doubled c-pawns that become a long-term weakness. Black follows up with ...c5 to challenge the center and targets the isolated c-pawns. This is the ultimate equalizing weapon in the QGD — simple to play, hard to crack, and it has neutralized many a strong White player.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 Rab1 Ng6 b4 a6 a4 Bd6 b5
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d5 early to create a symmetrical pawn structure, then launches the minority attack with b4-b5. Black must defend accurately on the queenside while seeking kingside counterplay. The key defensive idea is ...Nf8-g6, rerouting the knight to support both flanks. Despite the symmetry, these positions are rich with strategic nuance — Black should aim for piece activity on the kingside with ...Ng6, ...Bd6, and potentially ...Nf4.

variations.name: Ragozin Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Bb4 Bg5 dxc4 e4 c5 Bxc4 cxd4 Nxd4 Qa5 Bd2 Qc5
variations.explanation: The Ragozin blends ideas from the Nimzo-Indian and QGD — Black plays ...Bb4 pinning the knight and creating immediate tension. After ...dxc4 and ...c5, Black gets dynamic piece play and challenges White's center directly. The resulting positions are sharper than the classical QGD, with Black often getting counterplay against White's king if it castles queenside. This variation rewards players who like active, concrete play over slow maneuvering.

variations.name: Vienna Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 dxc4 e3 a6 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7 O-O Nbd7 e4 c5 d5 e5 b3
variations.explanation: The Vienna Variation features Black's early ...dxc4 capture, conceding the center temporarily but gaining time to develop the queenside with ...a6, ...b5, and ...Bb7. White gets a strong center but Black's queenside expansion creates counterplay. The resulting positions are complex and double-edged, with Black aiming for the ...c5 break to undermine White's center. This is a practical choice for players who prefer concrete play to theoretical battles.

variations.name: Cambridge Springs
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Nbd7 Nf3 c6 e3 Qa5 Nd2 Bb4 Qc2 O-O Bd3 dxc4
variations.explanation: The Cambridge Springs with ...Qa5 is a sharp counterattacking system where Black exploits the pin on the Nc3 along the a5-e1 diagonal. After ...Bb4 intensifies the pin, White faces difficult decisions about how to untangle. Black gets active piece play and targets White's center, with the queen on a5 coordinating beautifully with the bishop on b4 and knight on d7. This variation has been a reliable weapon for over a century and remains dangerous at all levels.

variations.name: Bf4 QGD
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bf4 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 c5 Nh5 Bd3 Nxf4 exf4 b6 b4 a5 a3 Ba6
variations.explanation: When White avoids Bg5 entirely and plays Bf4, the character of the game changes significantly. Black doesn't face the pin on the Nf6, so the light-squared bishop problem is less acute. Black can aim for ...Nh5 to challenge the Bf4 directly, or develop with ...b6 and ...Ba6 exchanging light-squared bishops. The resulting positions are quieter but strategically rich, with play revolving around the queenside pawn structure and the fight for control of e5.

name: Queen's Gambit Accepted
pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 Qe2 b5 Bb3 Bb7 Rd1 Nbd7 Nc3 Be7 e4 cxd4 Nxd4 Qb8 Bxe6 fxe6 Nxe6 Qe5 Nxg7+ Kf7 f4 Qc5+ Be3 Qc4 Qxc4+ bxc4
overview: The Queen's Gambit Accepted is a pragmatic and dynamic choice that dates back to chess's earliest analyzed positions (1490s Spanish manuscripts). Vladimir Steinitz analyzed it deeply in his championship matches; Alexander Alekhine used it as Black against Capablanca in their 1927 World Championship match; modern champions Vishy Anand and Veselin Topalov have played it consistently at the elite level — Anand famously won with it in his 2010 World Championship match against Topalov. You take the pawn on c4 not to hold it, but to develop freely while White recaptures. After …e6 and …c5, Black gets an active position with easy piece development.
keyIdeas: Take on c4 to free your position, then develop with ...e6, ...c5, and ...Nf6
The ...b5 push gains queenside space and prepares ...Bb7 on the long diagonal
After ...c5, Black challenges White's center directly and gets dynamic play
Don't try to hold the c4 pawn — develop pieces instead
traps: After e3 e6 Bxc4 c5, if White plays d5 too early, Black gets ...exd5 Bxd5 Nxd5 Qxd5 with a great position.
If White delays recapturing on c4, Black can play ...b5 and ...Bb7 to keep the pawn with a good game.
